items.question: Who uses pandas groupby and merge in an ML pipeline?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers use groupby to build entity-level summaries and merge to attach dimension tables—after loading and cleaning data and before or during feature creation for model training.

items.question: What is the split-apply-combine pattern in pandas groupby?
items.answer: Rows are split into groups by key column(s), a function (sum, mean, custom) is applied independently to each group, and results are combined into a Series or DataFrame—either one row per group (agg) or aligned to original rows (transform).

items.question: When should you use transform instead of agg after groupby?
items.answer: Use transform when each original row needs a group statistic broadcast back (same row count)—for example demeaning sales within store—use agg when you need one summary row per group to merge as entity-level features.

items.question: Where does join leakage typically enter a pandas ML workflow?
items.answer: Leakage enters when groupby stats are computed on the full dataset before train/test split, when label tables are merged into features too early, or when merges attach future or post-outcome columns by time or ID.

items.question: Why does a left merge differ from an inner merge for fact tables?
items.answer: Left merge keeps every row from the left (fact) table and fills NaN where the right table has no match; inner merge drops left rows without a match, which can silently shrink and bias the training sample.

items.question: How do you compute group features without join leakage?
items.answer: Split data first (or iterate CV folds), run groupby aggregations on training rows only, map or merge those train-learned stats onto train and test partitions, and never refit aggregates using validation or test rows.

items.question: How does inner join differ from left join in pd.merge?
items.answer: Inner join returns only rows with keys present in both tables, dropping unmatched rows from either side. Left join returns all left rows, attaching right columns where keys match and NaN where they do not—preserving the left table's row count.

items.question: How does groupby agg differ from groupby transform?
items.answer: agg reduces each group to one or more summary values (shorter or equal index length per group key), producing entity-level tables for merges. transform returns a Series the same length as the input, repeating each group's statistic on every member row for row-level features.
